text: Atom trapping generates a novel class of ceria-based catalysts featuring atomically dispersed Ru₁O₅ sites on (100) facets, as confirmed by spectroscopy and DFT calculations. These single-atom materials exhibit ruthenium properties that differ fundamentally from traditional metal/ceria systems, offering high efficiency in catalytic NO oxidation—a process that typically necessitates high loadings of expensive platinum-group metals in diesel aftertreatment. Ru₁/CeO₂ maintains exceptional stability throughout continuous thermal cycling, ramping, and moisture exposure. Furthermore, the catalyst demonstrates superior NOₓ storage capabilities at low temperatures, driven by the formation of stable Ru-NO complexes and a high rate of NOₓ spill-over onto the CeO₂ support; remarkably, as little as 0.05 wt % Ru is sufficient for effective storage. Unlike RuO₂ nanoparticles, which are prone to volatilization, the Ru₁O₅ sites remain stable during air/steam calcination up to 750 °C. By integrating DFT calculations with in situ DRIFTS and mass spectroscopy, we pinpointed the Ru(II) ions on the ceria surface and elucidated the specific mechanisms governing NO storage and oxidation. Additionally, Ru₁/CeO₂ facilitates efficient NO reduction by CO at low temperatures, achieving high activity with only 0.1–0.5 wt % Ru. Modulation-excitation in situ infrared and XPS measurements reveal the elementary steps of this reduction process, highlighting the catalyst's unique ability to generate oxygen vacancies and Ce³⁺ sites essential for low-temperature NO abatement. These findings underscore the potential of single-atom ruthenium catalysts as a robust and cost-effective solution for environmental exhaust treatment.
